original_abstract: As new academic journals emerge and submission rates climb, editors in political science have become increasingly concerned about "reviewer fatigue." Many assume that an overwhelming volume of requests has made scholars less willing to perform the anonymous, time-consuming service of manuscript evaluation. However, there has been little systematic research into why scholars actually decline these invitations. Using data from the *American Political Science Review*, we empirically investigated the rates at which scholars accept or decline requests, alongside their stated reasons for doing so. Our findings suggest that reviewer fatigue is only one of many factors influencing these decisions. The evidence indicates that while scholars remain generally willing to contribute to the peer-review process, they are often constrained by the competing demands of their professional and personal lives.
humanized_abstract: Reviewing papers for new journals is becoming a growing challenge for journal editors and many have recently begun to bemoan “reviewer fatigue.” As new journals come online and paper submissions to current journals are increasing, it is easy to assume that overwhelmed scholars decline requests to review or referee papers due to an excessive number of requests. This is largely a guess however and no one to my knowledge has yet empirically investigated why scholars decline a request to serve as an anonymous reviewer or referee. I’ve gotten some interesting data on this from reviewing requests for papers at the *American Political Science Review* (APSR). It turns out that reviewer fatigue is merely one possible factor in the scholars’ decision whether or not to agree to review/referee a paper, and only a small part of the APSR pool of potential reviewers have ever declined to agree to do so in my experience. Having a few more numbers, I had our administrative assistant do a survey of all recent requests for reviewers/review teams we made to find out what scholars say has led them to decline any such request. Turned out they remain quite willing to do this work — they just often decline doing so because of … life.
